odi.title: Preferred creditor treatment and multilateral development banks’ mobilisation agenda
odi.excerpt: The section also  addresses MDB guarantees on sovereign loans and investments in  MDB share capital. 5.1 Risk transfers  MDBs have increasingly sought to manage their balance sheets and  catalyse new investment through risk transfers. These allow MDBs to  shift the risk of their loans not being repaid to third parties – private  insurers, investors or official donors – in return for a fee.

odi.title: Country platforms in the context of rising sovereign debt levels
odi.authors: ["Archie Gilmour", "Shandelle Steadman", "Amy Cano Prentice", "Sima Kammourieh", "Sarah Colenbrander", "Martin Kessler"]
odi.excerpt: Equity is a transfer rewarded by co-ownership of the asset or company, and therefore the right  to receive a proportion of its revenues or profits once lenders have been repaid. Equity investors  face higher risks than debt investors because all loans must be repaid before they see any  returns – but they also do not have any limits to the possible returns in case of project success.

odi.excerpt: In all the MDBs, China invariably aligns with the  positions of the borrowing countries against  other donors, reflecting broader norms around  South–South cooperation – even in the many  MDBs where China is a creditor rather than a  borrower shareholder.

policy_center.title: India–East Africa A Not So Healthy Relationship?
policy_center.excerpt: Indian companies seeking to venture abroad make their decisions  based on their own interests and resorting to their own resources, but they  may also get support from India’s Exim Bank in the form of loans and  guarantees as well as equity finance. In some cases, Exim Bank has direct  participation in equity along with Indian promoters.
